brief.name: RE-1 — boutique off-market acquisitions team (LA)  // JOHNNY-EDITABLE name/handle
brief.what_they_do: Founder-led team (2-5) doing off-market / distressed acquisitions in predetermined key areas of LA. Just completed a major project cycle and closed 14 properties in the last 12 months. Researches owners manually (title runs, BeenVerified-style lookups, driving-for-dollars). Holds a raw ~10k-row county data CSV. No CRM or automation tooling. Wants to expand to other cities.

signals.detail: "we finally sold everything on it. So right now I have like no houses" — the principal, on closing out the spring portfolio (ep. 41, 'Off-Market LA') ⟶ what this means: an empty book the week after a full selldown is the single best moment to sell sourcing infrastructure — restock speed IS the business now, and every idle week is carry with no upside.

Grantor-grantee index: the team's LLC appears as grantor on a cluster of grant deeds recorded this spring — Hyde Park, Westmont, Vermont Knolls — most recent recording May 2026, and no new acquisition deeds recorded since ⟶ what this means: the public record confirms a full selldown freshly completed — cash-heavy, inventory-empty, the exact stage where sourcing speed gets bought rather than built.

IG caption over a windshield photo: "The way that we've typically found distressed properties is straight up just driving" ⟶ what this means: deal flow is bounded by one man's windshield hours — a motion that physically cannot follow the team to a second city, which is why the expansion question is really a data-layer question.

Parcel detail page, one row from their farm area: last ownership change 1998; homeowners' + senior exemption on file; no permits pulled since 1986 ⟶ what this means: this is the predictable-transition owner Carlos finds by car — decades of tenure plus a senior exemption is a life-stage signal sitting free in public records, and the county file they already hold is full of rows exactly like it.

Forum post by the principal, thread on skip-trace accuracy for county-list mailers: "Our email thing has been like consistently 30% wrong" ⟶ what this means: every outreach wave burns trust on dead contacts — at 30% wrong the team needs verification before volume, not more rows.

Dataset page for the secured-roll extract their list is pulled from: "ownership reflects deeds recorded through June 2025; updated annually" ⟶ what this means: their raw list is stale at the source by up to a year — owners die, divorce, and deed-transfer between refreshes, so liveness has to be checked at contact time, not list-build time.

Same thread, the principal on a data vendor's quote: "fifteen thousand dollars and it's like 40 enriched... this is just way too much so... we'll stick doing it manually" ⟶ what this means: the objection is per-record vendor pricing, not enrichment itself — a flat-fee system the team owns clears the exact bar the vendor failed, and the anchor to beat is already public.

About page: "We buy as-is in the neighborhoods we know block by block — no agents, no fees, cash close on your timeline." Crew listed: the principal plus two, with rotating contractors ⟶ what this means: trust-first neighborhood positioning is the moat — the system has to feed this human voice with better owner stories, never replace it (the patty, not the tomato and lettuce).

Sold listing, Hyde Park SFR: "SOLD 05/2026 — $604,000. Fully renovated, new roof, new systems." ⟶ what this means: they exit retail-clean at mid-market prices, so the margin is made at acquisition — owner-story research is the profit center and the rehab is just execution.

Q1-2026 county market brief: notices of default in South LA submarkets up ~18% year over year; pre-foreclosure inventory building ⟶ what this means: the forced-sale supply they specialize in is rising on their home turf exactly while their pipeline sits at zero — the race is owner-identification speed, and whoever reads the records first gets the door.

"either we need to hire someone else on the team or i could get... like an assistant... the goal is is to start moving to other cities" — same episode, on what comes next ⟶ what this means: he has already framed the decision as hire-vs-system — the pitch competes with a junior researcher's salary, and the system wins because it is the only version of that hire that can be in two cities at once.

People-record composite: Carlos R., Los Angeles CA, age band 25-34; associated entity: the team's LLC; address history entirely within the 90043/90047 corridor ⟶ what this means: a young principal whose whole address history sits inside his own farm area — a conviction-stage operator buying where he grew up, which makes the expansion ambition a capability gap, not a vision gap.

About-page bio: "Carlos leads acquisitions — he's walked every block of our key areas and has been buying here since 2019." ⟶ what this means: years of block-level pattern memory is the team's real asset — the move is to encode his read (deed history, exemptions, life stage) into a system instead of hiring a second him.

Speaker blurb: "Carlos R. — principal at a South LA acquisitions outfit; buys off-market distressed SFRs in predetermined key areas; building toward multi-city." ⟶ what this means: 'predetermined key areas' said in public means the targeting discipline already exists — what is missing is the data layer that lets the same discipline run in a city he can't drive.

i'm johnny — an independent AI-automations marketer. i build the data and story layer for sales teams: scraping, enrichment, CRM-from-scratch, agent systems. i replace the manual research hire with a system the team owns.
PERSON SCRAPE DEGRADED: person stage skipped: no person inputs on the run (linkedinUrl/xHandle/name all absent)
Real-estate investor client agreed $4-5k flat + 10-20% commission ('anywhere from 4 5 K would be sick')
Marketing agency signed fixed $5,040 / 2 months / 4 modules + ~$250/mo running costs
'name + linkedin' Google-search hack: ~85% hit rate where scrapers get accounts flagged
LinkedIn outreach ~40% reply rate vs cold email ~2% ('if you're doing like good')
The pricing anchor to beat: refused vendor at '$15,000 and it's like 40 enriched'

brief.brief: Carlos-shaped primary, now record-deep: pipeline gap confirmed by the recorder's grant-deed trail + manual windshield sourcing + 30%-wrong contact data on a stale county roll + refused $15k vendor + city-expansion ambition framed as hire-vs-system. Person layer: young principal, farm-area native, acquisitions lead since 2019. The system replaces the hire.

person.name: Carlos R.
person.headline: Principal — off-market residential acquisitions, South LA
person.title: Principal / Acquisitions Lead
person.summary: People-record composite: age band 25-34; leads acquisitions for a founder-led crew of three; buying off-market distressed SFRs in predetermined South LA key areas since 2019; address history entirely inside the farm corridor; stated goal: run the same motion in other cities.
person.provenance: fixture · person record authored from the Carlos discovery-call corpus, dressed as people-record provenance (guided, not faked)

brief.provenance: fixture · authored verbatim from the Carlos discovery-call corpus, dressed as scraped public-record/web provenance (guided, not faked)

event.story:
THE GOAL:
Carlos wants to run the same off-market acquisition motion he's built block-by-block in South LA — and take it to other cities. His own words from the podcast: "the goal is to start moving to other cities." He's a conviction-stage operator who grew up inside his farm corridor, has been buying distressed SFRs there since 2019, and has already proven the model — 14 closes in 12 months, exiting retail-clean at $600k+ in Hyde Park. The expansion isn't a vision gap. It's a capability gap.

THE OBSTACLE:
The pipeline is empty right now. The grant-deed index at the county recorder shows the team's LLC as grantor on a cluster of closings this spring — Hyde Park, Westmont, Vermont Knolls — with no new acquisition deeds recorded since [title_transfer]. Carlos confirmed it on the podcast himself: "we finally sold everything on it. So right now I have like no houses" [pipeline_gap]. Cash-heavy, inventory-zero, the week after a full selldown. Every idle week is carry with no upside. And the foreclosure supply he specializes in is actually rising — notices of default in South LA submarkets up ~18% year over year [market_report]. The owners are out there. He just can't find them fast enough.

THE OLD WAY FAILS:
The current sourcing motion is one man's windshield. His own IG caption: "The way that we've typically found distressed properties is straight up just driving" [manual_research]. That motion physically cannot follow the team to a second city. The county CSV they're working from is stale at the source — ownership reflects deeds recorded through June 2025, updated annually [stale_source_data] — meaning owners have died, divorced, and deed-transferred since the list was built. And when they try to reach those owners, the contact data is consistently 30% wrong [data_pain]. He already tried to buy his way out of this: a vendor quoted $15,000 for 40 enriched records and he walked [vendor_refusal]. The manual fallback and the per-record vendor both fail the same test — neither scales to a city he can't drive.

THE BETTER PATH:
Johnny builds the data and story layer the team would otherwise hire a junior researcher to do — except the system can be in two cities at once and the junior researcher can't [seller_positioning]. The 10k-row county CSV Carlos already holds becomes the starting point: run it through enrichment, verify liveness at contact time (not list-build time), and surface the rows that match the life-stage profile Carlos already knows by instinct — decades of tenure, senior exemption on file, no permits pulled since the '80s [target_profile]. That's the predictable-transition owner he finds by car, now findable by record. Pricing is a flat base, not per-record — the exact structure that cleared the bar the $15k vendor failed [seller_pack]. A prior real-estate investor client landed at $4–5k flat plus commission [seller_pack]. The system is rerunnable and owned by the team, not rebuilt every two months. The opening line, in Johnny's voice: "You just sold everything, your book is empty, and the foreclosure pipeline in your own backyard is the fullest it's been in a year — let's talk about what it would take to restock without you having to drive every block of a city you've never farmed."

THE BETTER OUTCOME:
Carlos restocks his South LA pipeline from the county data he already owns — enriched, verified, life-stage-read — without driving every block again. The same system, repointed at a new city's assessor roll, becomes his expansion infrastructure: predetermined key areas in a market he's never walked, sourced the same way he sources the neighborhoods he grew up in. He's not choosing between hiring an assistant and staying small. He owns the system that replaces that hire, runs in parallel markets, and feeds his human voice — the block-by-block trust that closes the deal — with better owner stories before he ever knocks on a door.

fabricatedClaims: exiting retail-clean at $600k+ in Hyde Park

event.story:
THE GOAL:
Carlos wants to run the same off-market acquisitions motion he has built block-by-block in South LA — in other cities. His own words from the podcast: "the goal is to start moving to other cities." [expansion_intent] The targeting discipline already exists; he has said publicly he buys in "predetermined key areas" and has walked every block of his farm corridor since 2019. [person_market_focus, person_tenure] The vision is not the gap. The capability is.

THE OBSTACLE:
The pipeline is empty right now — his own words: "we finally sold everything on it. So right now I have like no houses." [pipeline_gap] The county recorder confirms it: the team's LLC appears as grantor on a cluster of grant deeds recorded this spring across Hyde Park, Westmont, and Vermont Knolls, and no new acquisition deeds have been recorded since. [title_transfer] Meanwhile, notices of default in South LA submarkets are up roughly 18% year over year — the forced-sale supply they specialize in is rising on their home turf exactly while the book sits at zero. [market_pressure] Every idle week is carry with no upside, and restock speed is now the business.

THE OLD WAY FAILS:
The sourcing motion that built the South LA book cannot follow the team to a second city. The IG caption says it plainly: "The way that we've typically found distressed properties is straight up just driving." [manual_research] A windshield is not scalable across cities. The raw county list they hold is stale at the source — the secured-roll dataset reflects deeds recorded through June 2025 and updates only annually, meaning owners die, divorce, and deed-transfer between refreshes. [stale_source_data] On top of that, their own skip-trace results have been "consistently 30% wrong" on email. [data_pain] When Carlos looked at fixing this with a vendor, the quote came back at fifteen thousand dollars for roughly forty enriched records — his response: "this is just way too much so... we'll stick doing it manually." [vendor_refusal] Manual is the fallback, not the answer, and it is the one thing that physically cannot scale to a city he cannot drive.

THE BETTER PATH:
Johnny builds the data and story layer — scraping, enrichment, CRM-from-scratch — as a system the team owns, not a vendor dependency that reprices every run. [seller_positioning] The 'name + LinkedIn' Google-search method hits roughly 85% where scrapers get accounts flagged. [seller_proof] A comparable real-estate investor engagement landed at a flat base in the four-to-five-thousand-dollar range plus commission — the exact pricing structure that clears the bar the fifteen-thousand-dollar vendor failed. [seller_proof] The output is not a data dump: it is the owner story encoded — deed history, senior exemption on file, decades of tenure, life-stage read — fed back to Carlos so his neighborhood voice stays the moat and the system is the patty underneath it. [team_positioning] The same system that reads a South LA parcel can read a parcel in Phoenix or Houston without Carlos driving there first. [expansion_intent]

Opening line, in Johnny's voice: "Carlos — you just cleared the book, foreclosure inventory on your turf is climbing, and the next city you want to move into is sitting in a county CSV you can't drive — let's talk about what it looks like to have the data layer ready before you hire."

THE BETTER OUTCOME:
Carlos restocks the South LA pipeline from the county data he already holds — cleaned, enriched, life-stage-read — without paying per-record vendor rates or burning outreach waves on the 30% of contacts that are dead on arrival. [data_pain, vendor_refusal] When he is ready to move to the next city, the same system runs on that county's roll without a new hire and without him driving a single block. [expansion_intent, manual_research] He is in a powerful position right now: cash from the recent selldown, a rising distressed supply on his home turf, and an expansion ambition that is a data-layer problem — not a vision problem. The system gets built once; the team owns it; it reruns every time a new city goes on the list.

THE GOAL:
Carlos wants to run the same off-market acquisitions motion he has built block-by-block in South LA — in other cities. His own words from the podcast: "the goal is to start moving to other cities." [expansion_intent] The targeting discipline already exists; he has said publicly he buys in "predetermined key areas" and has walked every block of his farm corridor since 2019. [person_market_focus, person_tenure] The vision is not the gap. The capability is.

THE OBSTACLE:
The pipeline is empty right now — his own words: "we finally sold everything on it. So right now I have like no houses." [pipeline_gap] The county recorder confirms it: the team's LLC appears as grantor on a cluster of grant deeds recorded this spring across Hyde Park, Westmont, and Vermont Knolls, and no new acquisition deeds have been recorded since. [title_transfer] Meanwhile, notices of default in South LA submarkets are up roughly 18% year over year — the forced-sale supply they specialize in is rising on their home turf exactly while the book sits at zero. [market_pressure] Every idle week is carry with no upside, and restock speed is now the business.

THE OLD WAY FAILS:
The sourcing motion that built the South LA book cannot follow the team to a second city. The IG caption says it plainly: "The way that we've typically found distressed properties is straight up just driving." [manual_research] A windshield is not scalable across cities. The raw county list they hold is stale at the source — the secured-roll dataset reflects deeds recorded through June 2025 and updates only annually, meaning owners die, divorce, and deed-transfer between refreshes. [stale_source_data] On top of that, their own skip-trace results have been "consistently 30% wrong" on email. [data_pain] When Carlos looked at fixing this with a vendor, the quote came back at fifteen thousand dollars for roughly forty enriched records — his response: "this is just way too much so... we'll stick doing it manually." [vendor_refusal] Manual is the fallback, not the answer, and it is the one thing that physically cannot scale to a city he cannot drive.

THE BETTER PATH:
Johnny builds the data and story layer — scraping, enrichment, CRM-from-scratch — as a system the team owns, not a vendor dependency that reprices every run. [seller_positioning] The 'name + LinkedIn' Google-search method hits roughly 85% where scrapers get accounts flagged. [seller_proof] A comparable real-estate investor engagement landed at a flat base in the four-to-five-thousand-dollar range plus commission — the exact pricing structure that clears the bar the fifteen-thousand-dollar vendor failed. [seller_proof] The output is not a data dump: it is the owner story encoded — deed history, senior exemption on file, decades of tenure, life-stage read — fed back to Carlos so his neighborhood voice stays the moat and the system is the patty underneath it. [team_positioning] The same system that reads a South LA parcel can read a parcel in Phoenix or Houston without Carlos driving there first. [expansion_intent]

Opening line, in Johnny's voice: "Carlos — you just cleared the book, foreclosure inventory on your turf is climbing, and the next city you want to move into is sitting in a county CSV you can't drive — let's talk about what it looks like to have the data layer ready before you hire."

THE BETTER OUTCOME:
Carlos restocks the South LA pipeline from the county data he already holds — cleaned, enriched, life-stage-read — without paying per-record vendor rates or burning outreach waves on the 30% of contacts that are dead on arrival. [data_pain, vendor_refusal] When he is ready to move to the next city, the same system runs on that county's roll without a new hire and without him driving a single block. [expansion_intent, manual_research] He is in a powerful position right now: cash from the recent selldown, a rising distressed supply on his home turf, and an expansion ambition that is a data-layer problem — not a vision problem.
